planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist to slightly dry, well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; southern shield fern is exceptionally heat and drought tolerant for a fern once established and is one of the most reliable shade ferns for Gulf Coast and Deep South gardens.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade — southern shield fern (Thelypteris kunthii) is the workhorse fern of the Gulf Coast and Southeast, naturalized along stream banks, drainage ditches, woodland edges, and the shaded understory of pine and live oak forests from Virginia to Texas. It spreads by creeping rhizomes to form large colonies, deciduous in the upper zones but semi-evergreen in zone 9–10, and tolerates more heat, drought, and root competition from large trees than virtually any other native fern in the South. The fronds are medium green, 2–3 feet tall, with pinnate-pinnatifid division and a soft texture; they emerge in spring and provide lush, layered textural coverage all season. Establish with regular water, then let it fend for itself — a uniquely low-maintenance shade groundcover for difficult dry-shade situations under mature trees. Remove old fronds in late winter before new growth emerges.

planting.note: Plant divisions in fall or early spring in moist to moderately dry, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; southern wild ginger is slow to spread but forms attractive evergreen colonies and is more heat and drought tolerant than the northern wild ginger (A. canadense), making it the better choice for zones 7–9.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade — southern wild ginger is an elegant native groundcover of the southeastern US Piedmont and coastal plain, producing attractive, arrowhead-shaped evergreen leaves that are beautifully mottled with silver-gray markings along the veins. Unlike its deciduous northern relative (Asarum canadense), this species holds its foliage through winter in zones 7–9, maintaining year-round groundcover. The curious jug-shaped, brown-purple flowers emerge at soil level in early spring and are pollinated by ground beetles — they are almost entirely hidden by the foliage and go unnoticed, but the effect in leaf is outstanding. The foliage has a mild gingery fragrance when crushed. It spreads slowly by rhizome to form dense mats that suppress weeds under trees and in shaded beds, making it an excellent choice for dry to moderately moist shade in southern gardens.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions at or just below the waterline or in consistently wet to moist soil in full sun to partial shade; sweet flag is a wetland plant and requires consistently wet feet — it will not thrive in dry or even moderately moist conditions. Can spread aggressively in ideal wet conditions.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions at the water's edge or in wet, mucky soil in full sun to partial shade — sweet flag is an ancient and widely distributed wetland plant with strongly aromatic, iris-like foliage and an equally fragrant rhizome (calamus root) used for centuries in perfumery, flavoring, and herbal medicine. The sword-like leaves are attractive throughout the growing season, forming upright clumps at pond and stream margins, in rain gardens, and in boggy low spots. The rhizome contains calamus oil (asarone) with a complex, spicy-sweet fragrance; the dried root was historically used as an herbal stimulant and flavoring but internal use is now discouraged due to beta-asarone content. It spreads vigorously in wet conditions by horizontal rhizomes; divide every 3–4 years to prevent overcrowding. The American diploid form (var. americanus) is considered native; the tetraploid form widely sold in commerce is introduced from Europe and Asia.

planting.note: Plant divisions in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; dwarf sweet flag tolerates a wide range of moisture conditions from constantly wet to moderately moist and is more shade-tolerant and smaller than the species calamus. The golden cultivar 'Ogon' is especially valued for brightening shaded areas.

directions: Plant divisions in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade — dwarf sweet flag is a compact, grass-like Japanese native widely used as a shade-tolerant groundcover, edging plant, and water garden marginal throughout zone 5–9 gardens. The narrow, arching leaves are evergreen in warmer zones and have a pleasant, sweet-spicy fragrance when brushed or crushed. The golden cultivar 'Ogon' (Ogon Japanese Sweet Flag) is one of the finest shade-brightening plants available: its year-round golden-chartreuse foliage creates a luminous effect in dark corners, along shaded walkways, or at the edge of reflecting pools. 'Pusillus' is an extremely miniature form only 2–3 inches tall used in terrariums and troughs. Tolerates wet feet and very moist conditions but adapts to moderately moist soil in shade; divide every 3–4 years to maintain vigor and spread.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in wet to moist, slightly acidic, fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; swamp spider lily thrives in pond margins, wet ditches, and boggy, shaded spots where few other bulbs will grow. It is winter-hardy to zone 7 and resprouts reliably from established bulbs.

directions: Plant bulbs in wet to moist, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun — the swamp spider lily is a spectacular Gulf Coast native wildflower found naturally in wet prairies, swampy ditches, stream banks, and the margins of cypress swamps from Texas to Georgia. In spring it produces clusters of 3–6 intensely fragrant, spider-like white flowers on leafless scapes 18–24 inches tall — the long, narrow, reflexed petals surrounding a prominent cup-like corona are architecturally striking and fill the garden with a rich, sweet perfume. The broad, strap-like leaves emerge in spring and die back by late summer. It naturalizes freely in wet conditions, forming spectacular colonies in roadside ditches and pond margins throughout the Gulf Coast region. Unlike many spider lilies, it thrives in partial shade and wet soils where most other bulbs fail. Excellent at the edge of rain gardens, retention ponds, and boggy low spots.

planting.note: Plant divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to dappled light; blue walking iris is tender below 28°F but resprouts from established clumps in zone 9 after light freezes. In zone 8 treat as a large container plant and overwinter indoors.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to filtered light — blue walking iris is one of the most dramatically beautiful foliage and flowering plants for the shaded Gulf Coast garden, producing bold, fan-shaped clumps of upright, iris-like leaves 3–4 feet tall. The large, intricately patterned blue-purple flowers — each 3–4 inches across with white and golden markings — open from plantlets produced at the tips of flower stems; when these plantlets become heavy enough, the stem bends to the ground and the offset roots to start a new plant nearby, 'walking' the clump gradually across the garden. The flowers last only a single day but appear in succession over several weeks in late spring and early summer. An outstanding large-scale accent or specimen plant for the shaded side of buildings, under oaks, and in tropical-style garden compositions in zone 9–11. Grow in containers in cooler zones.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in water or moist potting mix at any time in warm weather; in zone 10–11 plant out after nighttime temperatures are consistently above 55°F. In zone 9 plant in a sheltered, frost-protected spot or use as a container plant brought inside before frost.

directions: Root stem cuttings in water and plant in moist, well-drained, fertile soil in partial to deep shade — arrowhead plant is one of the most adaptable and beautiful shade foliage plants in the world, naturalizing as a groundcover or scrambling vine in zone 10–12 and widely used as a container or houseplant in cooler zones. Young plants produce distinctive arrowhead-shaped leaves that gradually shift to deeply lobed, multi-part leaves as the plant matures and begins to climb or vine. The many cultivars offer an extraordinary range of leaf patterns: solid green, pink, cream-variegated, pale green-silver, and deep burgundy forms are all widely grown. Syngonium thrives in the deep shade of buildings, porches, and mature tree canopies where it provides lush, jungle-like texture. It roots readily wherever stems contact moist soil, spreading to colonize shaded areas in tropical climates; it is considered invasive in Florida and Hawaii when grown outdoors.

planting.note: Plant divisions in fall or early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade; Indian pink is slow to establish but long-lived and low-maintenance once settled. It resents root disturbance — plant at its final location and leave undisturbed.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to light shade — Indian pink is one of the most spectacular hummingbird plants in the eastern native flora, producing clusters of brilliant red tubular flowers with abruptly flared, yellow-green tips in late spring to early summer, providing perfectly shaped perches and nectar reservoirs for ruby-throated hummingbirds. The intensely two-toned coloration — deep red tube, bright yellow mouth — makes it one of the most visually striking wildflowers in the woodland garden. It grows naturally in the moist, humus-rich soils of deciduous forest understories and shaded ravines from Maryland to Kansas and south to Florida. The upright, multi-stemmed clumps produce shiny, dark green, opposite leaves and bloom reliably in dappled shade. Slow to establish but extremely long-lived; pairs beautifully with wild blue phlox, wild columbine, and maidenhair fern in the shade garden.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist potting mix or plant container plants after nighttime temperatures are consistently above 55°F; golden shrimp plant establishes quickly in warm weather and begins blooming within 6–8 weeks of transplanting.

directions: Root cuttings or plant nursery transplants in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in partial shade to filtered sun — golden shrimp plant is one of the most continuously showy flowering plants for shaded Gulf Coast gardens, producing upright spikes of overlapping golden-yellow bracts with small white tubular flowers emerging from the tips, blooming nearly year-round in frost-free climates from late spring through fall. The bright golden cones create striking color in shaded beds, under trees, and along north-facing walls where few other flowering plants perform well. Pinch stem tips regularly in the first month after planting to encourage bushy growth; established plants can be cut back by half in late winter to rejuvenate. In zone 9, top growth is killed by frost but plants often resprout from roots if the freeze is brief; in zone 10–11 it grows as a permanent landscape shrub. An outstanding container plant for shaded patios and screened porches.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall or divide clumps in spring; spiked wood oats is more shade and drought tolerant than the related inland sea oats (Chasmanthium latifolium) and performs well in dry to moderately moist conditions in full to partial shade.

directions: Direct sow in fall or plant divisions in spring in dry to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — spiked wood oats is a fine-textured native grass of the southeastern and south-central US, closely related to inland sea oats (C. latifolium) but notably more drought and shade tolerant and somewhat smaller and narrower in leaf. It grows naturally in dry, rocky, or sandy woodland soils under hardwood forest canopies and along dry bluffs and ridges, where its minimal water requirements make it a standout among shade grasses. The attractive seed heads emerge in summer, erect rather than pendulous, and turn a warm tan by fall, providing winter interest and wildlife food. An excellent grass for filling dry shade under oaks and other trees where irrigation is unavailable, or for naturalizing in shaded woodland garden settings. Self-sows moderately; cut back to the ground in late winter to encourage fresh growth.
